exercises.title: Why the pipeline has a latency floor
exercises.prompt: Explain why a classic ASR→LLM→TTS voice pipeline has a fundamental latency floor (~300-500ms) no amount of tuning removes, and what Moshi's full-duplex approach replaces it with.
exercises.hint: Each stage has a minimum latency and they're chained; full-duplex is one model, audio-in to audio-out, no pipeline boundaries.
exercises.solution:
A pipelined agent runs **VAD → STT → LLM → TTS** as distinct stages, each with its own irreducible minimum latency (STT needs some audio to transcribe, the LLM needs time to first token, TTS needs startup). Even with perfect streaming and overlap, the **pipeline *shape*** — discrete stages with handoffs and end-pointing — imposes a floor around 300-500ms. You can tune and parallelize, but you can't remove the structural cost of converting audio→text→reasoning→text→audio across module boundaries.

**Moshi's answer: no pipeline.** A single model takes **audio in and emits audio out directly and continuously**, with text as an optional internal 'inner monologue' rather than a required intermediate stage. There's no separate STT/TTS to chain and no explicit turn-end handoff. Theoretical latency ~160ms (80ms Mimi frame + 80ms acoustic delay), ~200ms in practice on one L4 — roughly **half** the best pipelined agent — because the audio→audio mapping happens in one model over codec tokens, not across four modules.

exercises.title: Full-duplex: listening while speaking
exercises.prompt: Moshi is 'full-duplex' — it listens and speaks simultaneously. Explain how modeling two parallel audio streams (user + assistant) over codec tokens enables this, and what natural conversational behaviors it unlocks that a half-duplex pipeline cannot.
exercises.hint: It jointly models its own output stream and the user's input stream as parallel token streams; can react mid-utterance.
exercises.solution:
**Half-duplex** pipelines are strictly turn-based: listen, *then* speak, *then* listen — they can't do both at once, which is why barge-in needs bolted-on echo cancellation and why interruptions feel clunky.

**Moshi models two parallel streams of Mimi codec tokens simultaneously** — the **user's input audio** and **its own output audio** — predicting its next output tokens while continuously consuming the user's input tokens. Because both streams are jointly modeled at every step, the model is *always listening even while it's talking*.

**What this unlocks (impossible half-duplex):**
- **Natural barge-in:** it hears the user start talking over it and can stop/yield immediately — no separate AEC + interrupt logic.
- **Backchannels:** 'mm-hmm', 'right', 'go on' *while the user is still speaking* — the overlapping affirmations real conversations have.
- **No rigid turn-taking:** overlap, quick interjections, and fluid hand-offs, because there's no enforced 'your turn / my turn' gate.
- **Lower latency:** it can begin responding the instant it has enough, without waiting for a hard end-point.

The inner-monologue text stream keeps the content coherent (the model 'thinks in text' while generating audio). Modeling conversation as concurrent audio streams — not alternating turns — is what makes it feel human.

exercises.title: Hibiki and simultaneous translation
exercises.prompt: Hibiki does streaming speech-to-speech translation chunk-by-chunk on the same full-duplex architecture. Explain why simultaneous (real-time) translation is harder than batch translation, the quality-vs-latency tradeoff it must make, and how the codec-token approach helps.
exercises.hint: Must translate before the sentence is finished (word order differs across languages); waiting improves quality but adds latency.
exercises.solution:
**Why simultaneous translation is harder than batch:** batch translation sees the *whole* source sentence before producing output, so it can reorder freely. Real-time translation must **emit target audio while the source is still being spoken**, before the sentence is complete — but languages have **different word orders** (e.g. verb-final languages), so the correct early translation may depend on words not yet spoken. The translator must decide *when it has enough* to safely commit a chunk.

**The quality-vs-latency tradeoff:** **wait longer** (more source context) → better, more accurate translation but higher latency (the listener falls behind the speaker). **Commit sooner** → lower latency but risk mistranslating because a later word changes the meaning, forcing awkward corrections. Hibiki must continuously balance this 'read more vs write now' decision — the core problem of simultaneous interpretation.

**How codec tokens help:** operating over **Mimi codec tokens** in the same full-duplex framework lets Hibiki consume source-audio tokens and emit target-audio tokens **chunk-by-chunk in one model**, continuously — no STT→MT→TTS pipeline (which would compound latency and lose prosody). It can also **preserve voice/prosody** because it generates acoustic tokens directly, and it can adapt its emission pacing within the stream. The full-duplex, token-streaming design is exactly what a real-time translator needs: simultaneously ingest one language's audio and produce another's, trading off context vs latency on the fly.
